title: Removing and replacing vinyl floor tiles
subtitle: Lift damaged tiles and fit matching replacements
excerpt: How to remove damaged or lifting vinyl floor tiles and fit new ones: softening the adhesive with a heat gun, lifting tiles cleanly, preparing the subfloor, and bonding replacement tiles with the right adhesive.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: The structural floor beneath the floor covering. In domestic buildings this is typically concrete screed, a wooden floorboard deck, or a sheet of hardboard or plywood. The subfloor must be flat, dry, and sound before any floor covering is fixed.

An adhesive applied to both surfaces being joined, allowed to become touch-dry, and then pressed together. The bond forms on contact with no clamping required. Used for vinyl tiles, sheet vinyl, and laminate edging.

content.text: Vinyl floor tiles lift and crack with age, with water ingress at joints, or after something heavy is dropped on them. Replacing individual tiles is straightforward if you can source a matching tile: check the original packaging or lift a tile from a hidden corner under a fitted appliance to compare. The trickiest part of the job is getting the old tile and its adhesive off the

Vinyl floor tiles made before 1985 may contain asbestos fibres in the tile or in the adhesive beneath. Do not sand, drill, or abrasively scrape any pre-1985 vinyl tile. If you are unsure of the age, treat the tiles as potentially containing asbestos and have a sample tested by an accredited laboratory before disturbing them.

A heat gun, a 100 mm filling knife, a 50 mm scraper, a utility knife, a sanding block with 80-grit paper, floor tile adhesive, and the replacement tiles. For self-adhesive tiles, use peel-and-stick pressure adhesive instead. Wear protective gloves when handling adhesive.
Step 1, soften and lift the damaged tile

Set the heat gun to its lower setting and hold it 150 mm above the damaged tile, moving it in slow circles. After 30 to 60 seconds the tile becomes pliable. Slide the filling knife under one corner and lever gently; the tile should peel away in sections. If it resists, apply more heat rather than more force. Work from one corner progressively across the tile.

Once the tile is removed, scrape the remaining adhesive from the subfloor with the 50 mm scraper, warming resistant patches with the heat gun. The subfloor must be flat, clean, and free of adhesive lumps before the replacement tile is fitted. Any ridges or dried adhesive will telegraph through the new tile and eventually crack it. Sand any raised areas with 80-grit on a sanding block.

Dry-lay the replacement tile and check it sits flat and flush with the surrounding tiles. For peel-and-stick tiles, peel the backing and press firmly down, then roll with a rolling pin to bed the adhesive fully. For tiles needing separate adhesive, apply
to the subfloor only (not the tile back), allow it to become touch-dry, then press the tile home. Roll with firm pressure to ensure full contact.

attrs.symptom: New tile lifting at edges within days
attrs.cause: Adhesive not fully touch-dry before tile was placed, or subfloor has residual moisture.
attrs.fix: Lift the tile carefully, allow the adhesive to cure fully, check the subfloor moisture level, and re-lay.

attrs.symptom: New tile sits proud of surrounding tiles
attrs.cause: Residual adhesive or a ridge on the subfloor beneath the replacement area.
attrs.fix: Lift the tile, scrape and sand the subfloor to a level surface, and re-lay.
